solution:
The general term in the expansion of a+bn is Tr+1=Crnan-rbr.

Given, in the expansion of 1+xlog2x5, third term is 2560

⇒T3= 5C2xlog2x2=2560

Using, Crn=n!r!·n-r!, we get

⇒5!2!·3!xlog2x2=2560

⇒5·4·3!2×1·3!x2log2x=2560

⇒x2log2x=256

Taking logarithm to the base 2 on both sides

⇒log2x2log2x=log2256

Now, using logamn=nlogam & logaa=1,

⇒2log2xlog2x=log228

⇒ 2log2x2=8

⇒log2x=±2

Using, logax=b, ⇒x=ab

x=22, 2-2

⇒x=4, 14

Here, only x=14 is given in the options.

question: In the binomial expansion of $(a-b)^n, n \geq 5$, the sum of $5^{\text {th }}$ and $6^{\text {th }}$ terms is zero, then $\frac{a}{b}$ equals
solution:
${ }^n C_4 a^{n-4}(-b)^4+{ }^n C_5 a^{n-5}(-b)^5=0$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)=\frac{n-5+1}{5}$.

question: If the constant term, in binomial expansion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mi>r</mi></msup><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is equal to ____________.
solution:
The general term in the binomial expansion of a+bn is CRnan-RbR.

Thus, the general term in the expansion of 2xr+1x210 is =CR102xr10-R1x2R

=CR10210-Rxr10-Rx-2R

Given, the constant term in the expansion is 180

⇒CR10·210-R=180   …1 

And (10-R)r-2R=0

⇒r=2R10-R

⇒r=2(R-10)10-R+2010-R

⇒r=-2+2010-R    …2

Since R is positive integer less than or equal to 10 and r is also an integer, hence, the value of R is such that, 10-R must divide 20.

This is possible, when the value of 10-R can be one from the divisors of 20 i.e. 10-R can be from 1, 2, 4 or 5 and consequently, R can be 9, 8, 6 or 5 respectively.

But for R=9, 6 & 5 equation 1 is  not satisfied, hence R=8

as CR10210-R=180

⇒r=2×810-8=8.

q: For some $n \neq 10$, let the coefficients of the 5 th, 6 th and 7 th terms in the binomial expansion of $(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{n}+4}$ be in A.P. Then the largest coefficient in the expansion of $(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{n}+4}$ is:
question: For some $n \neq 10$, let the coefficients of the 5 th, 6 th and 7 th terms in the binomial expansion of $(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{n}+4}$ be in A.P. Then the largest coefficient in the expansion of $(1+\mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{n}+4}$ is:
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & (1+x)^{n+4} \\ & { }^{n+4} C_4,{ }^{n+4} C_5,{ }^{n+4} C_6, \rightarrow \text { A.P. } \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \times { }^{n+4} C_5={ }^{n+4} C_4+{ }^{n+4} C_6 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \times{ }^{n+4} C_5=\left({ }^{n+4} C_4+{ }^{n+4} C_5\right)+\left({ }^{n+4} C_5+{ }^{n+4} C_6\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \times{ }^{n+4} C_5={ }^{n+5} C_5+{ }^{n+5} C_6\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 4 \times \frac{(\mathrm{n}+4)!}{5!\cdot(\mathrm{n}-1)!}=\frac{(\mathrm{n}+6)!}{6!\cdot \mathrm{n}!} \\ & \Rightarrow 4=\frac{(\mathrm{n}+6)(\mathrm{n}+5)}{6 \mathrm{n}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^2+11 \mathrm{n}+30=24 \mathrm{n} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^2-13 \mathrm{n}+30=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3,10(\text { rejected }) \\ & \because \mathrm{n} \neq 10\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Largest binomial coefficient in expansion of
$\begin{aligned}
& (1+\mathrm{x})^7 \\
& (\because \mathrm{n}+4=7)
\end{aligned}$
is coeff. of middle term
$\Rightarrow{ }^7 \mathrm{C}_4={ }^7 \mathrm{C}_3=35$

q: Suppose <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow><mn>2023</mn></munderover><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>·</mo><mmultiscripts><mi>C</mi><mi>r</mi><none></none><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>2023</mn></mmultiscripts><mo>=</mo><mn>2023</mn><mo>×</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mn>2022</mn></msup></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is

solution:
Given,

∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=2023×α×22022

Now taking L.H.S we get,

∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=∑r=12023r2·2023r·Cr-12022

⇒∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=∑r=12023r·2023·Cr-12022

⇒∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=2023∑r=12023r-1+1·Cr-12022

⇒∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=2023∑r=12023r-1·Cr-12022+∑r=12023Cr-12022

⇒∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=20232022∑r=22023Cr-22021+∑r=12023Cr-12022

⇒∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=20232022×22021+22022

⇒∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=2023×220221011+1

⇒∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=2023×1012×22022

Now on comparing with ∑r=02023r2·Cr2023=2023×α×22022 we get,

α=1012

question: If the coefficients of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mi>p</mi></msup><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mi>q</mi></msup></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></math> respectively, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>p</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>q</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given that The coefficient of x and x2 in 1+xp1-xq are 4 and -5.

⇒1+xp1-xq=1+px+pp-12!x2+....1-qx+qq-12!x2-....

Now coefficient of x from the above expansion will be

p-q which is equal to 4

⇒p-q=4.

Similarly coefficient of x2 is -5.

⇒pp-12+qq-12-pq=-5

⇒p2-2pq+q22-p+q2=-5

⇒p-q22-p+q2=-5

⇒162+5=p+q2

⇒p+q=26 and p-q=4

On solving the above equations we get,

p=15 and q=11.

⇒2p+3q=215+311=63

q: Let the smallest value of $k \in \mathbb{N}$, for which the coefficient of $x^3$ in $(1+x)^3 + (1+x)^4 + (1+x)^5 + \ldots + (1+x)^{99} + (1+kx)^{100}$, $x \neq 0$, is $\left(43n + \dfrac{101}{4}\right)\left(^{100}C_3\right)$ for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$, be $p$. Then the value of $p + n$ is:
question: Let the smallest value of $k \in \mathbb{N}$, for which the coefficient of $x^3$ in $(1+x)^3 + (1+x)^4 + (1+x)^5 + \ldots + (1+x)^{99} + (1+kx)^{100}$, $x \neq 0$, is $\left(43n + \dfrac{101}{4}\right)\left(^{100}C_3\right)$ for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$, be $p$. Then the value of $p + n$ is:
solution:
The coefficient of $x^3$ in the given expression is the sum of the coefficients of $x^3$ in each term.

The coefficient of $x^3$ in $\sum_{r=3}^{99} (1+x)^r + (1+kx)^{100}$ is:

$\sum_{r=3}^{99} {}^{r}C_3 + k^3 {}^{100}C_3$

Using the identity $\sum_{r=k}^{n} {}^{r}C_k = {}^{n+1}C_{k+1}$, we get:

$\sum_{r=3}^{99} {}^{r}C_3 = {}^{100}C_4$

Thus, the total coefficient of $x^3$ is ${}^{100}C_4 + k^3 {}^{100}C_3$.

Given that this coefficient is equal to $\left(43n + \dfrac{101}{4}\right) {}^{100}C_3$, we have:

${}^{100}C_4 + k^3 {}^{100}C_3 = \left(43n + \dfrac{101}{4}\right) {}^{100}C_3$

Dividing both sides by ${}^{100}C_3$:

$\dfrac{^{100}C_4}{^{100}C_3} + k^3 = 43n + \dfrac{101}{4}$

Using $\dfrac{^{n}C_r}{^{n}C_{r-1}} = \dfrac{n-r+1}{r}$, we get $\dfrac{^{100}C_4}{^{100}C_3} = \dfrac{100-4+1}{4} = \dfrac{97}{4}$.

Substituting this value:

$\dfrac{97}{4} + k^3 = 43n + \dfrac{101}{4}$

$k^3 = 43n + \dfrac{101}{4} - \dfrac{97}{4}$

$k^3 = 43n + 1$

$k^3 - 1 = 43n$

Since $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we must have $n \ge 1$, which implies $k^3 - 1 \ge 43 \Rightarrow k \ge 4$.

Checking the values of $k \ge 4$ to find the smallest integer $k$ such that $k^3 - 1$ is divisible by $43$:

For $k = 4$, $k^3 - 1 = 63$ (not divisible by $43$).

For $k = 5$, $k^3 - 1 = 124$ (not divisible by $43$).

For $k = 6$, $k^3 - 1 = 215 = 43 \times 5$.

Thus, the smallest value of $k$ is $p = 6$, and the corresponding value of $n$ is $5$.

Therefore, $p + n = 6 + 5 = 11$.

Answer: $11$

Let the coefficients of the middle terms in the expansion of 16+βx4,1-3βx2 and 1-β2x6,β>0, respectively form the first three terms of an A.P. If d is the common difference of this A.P., then 50-2dβ2 is equal to _____ .

Given,

Coefficients of middle terms of given expansions 16+βx4, 1-3βx2 and1-β2x6 are C2416β2, C12-3β & C36-β23 respectively,

Now it is given that middle terms are forming an A.P

So, 2C12-3β = C36-β23+C2416β2

⇒2.2-3β=β2-5β32

⇒-24=2β-5β2

⇒5β2-2β-24=0

⇒5β2-12β+10β-24=0

⇒β5β-12+25β-12=0

⇒β+25β-12=0

So, β=125 and β=-2 {which is not possible as β>0}

Now d=C12-3β-C2416β2=-6β-β2

Now putting the value of β & d in 50-2dβ2 we get,

50-2dβ2=50-2-6β-β2β2=50+12β+2=57

solution:
The general term of multinomial expansion is
$\frac{6!}{\alpha!\beta!\gamma!}(1)^\alpha\left(2^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^\beta\left(3^{\frac{1}{2}}\right)^\gamma$

For terms to be rational $3 \mid \beta$ and $2 \mid \gamma$
$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\beta & \gamma & \alpha & \text { Term } \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 6 & 1 \\
0 & 2 & 4 & 15.3=45 \\
0 & 4 & 2 & 15.3^2=135 \\
0 & 6 & 0 & 1.3^3=27 \\
3 & 0 & 3 & 20.2=40 \\
3 & 2 & 1 & 60.2 .3=360 \\
6 & 0 & 0 & 1.4=4
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow$ Sum of rational terms
$=1+45+135+27+40+360+4=612$

q: The term independent of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(\frac{(x+1)}{\left(x^{2 / 3}+1-x^{1 / 3}\right)}-\frac{(x+1)}{\left(x-x^{1 / 2}\right)}\right)^{10}, x\gt1$ is:
question: The term independent of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(\frac{(x+1)}{\left(x^{2 / 3}+1-x^{1 / 3}\right)}-\frac{(x+1)}{\left(x-x^{1 / 2}\right)}\right)^{10}, x\gt1$ is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{(x+1)}{\left(x^{\frac{2}{3}}+1-x^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)}-\frac{(x-1)}{\left(x-x^{\frac{1}{2}}\right)}\right)^{10} \\ & =\left(\left(x^{\frac{1}{3}}+1\right)-\left(\frac{\sqrt{x}+1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)\right)^{10} \\ & =\left(x^{\frac{1}{3}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)^{10} \\ & T_{r+1}={ }^{10} C_r(x)^{\frac{10-r}{3}}(-1)^r(x)^{-\frac{r}{2}} \\ & \frac{10-r}{3}-\frac{r}{2}=0 \\ & (20-2 r)-3 r=0 \\ & r^{10}=4 \\ & \Rightarrow{ }^{10} C_4(-1)^4=210\end{aligned}$

q: The coefficient of $x^2$ in the expansion of $\left(2x^2 + \dfrac{1}{x}\right)^{10}$, $x \neq 0$, is :
question: The coefficient of $x^2$ in the expansion of $\left(2x^2 + \dfrac{1}{x}\right)^{10}$, $x \neq 0$, is :
solution:
The general term in the expansion of $\left(2x^2 + \dfrac{1}{x}\right)^{10}$ is given by:

$T_{r+1} = ^{10}C_{r} (2x^2)^{10-r} \left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)^r$

$T_{r+1} = ^{10}C_{r} 2^{10-r} x^{20-2r} x^{-r}$

$T_{r+1} = ^{10}C_{r} 2^{10-r} x^{20-3r}$

For the coefficient of $x^2$, we equate the power of $x$ to $2$:

$20 - 3r = 2$

$3r = 18 \Rightarrow r = 6$

Substituting $r = 6$ in the coefficient part, we get:

Coefficient $= ^{10}C_{6} 2^{10-6}$

$= ^{10}C_{4} 2^4$

$= \dfrac{10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7}{4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1} \times 16$

$= 210 \times 16$

$= 3360$

Answer: $3360$

question: If $\mathrm{x}$ is positive, the first negative term in the expansion of $(1+x)^{27 / 5}$ is
solution:
$T_{r+1}=\frac{n(n-1)(n-2) \ldots \ldots \ldots(n-r+1)}{r !}(x)^r$
For first negative term, $n-r+1 < 0$ or $r>\frac{32}{5}$
$\therefore \mathrm{r}=7$. Therefore, first negative term is $\mathrm{T}_8$.
